paragraphs.text: BUSINESS
UNIVERSITY
EAIF
Brussels, 24th of October 2025

The European AI Forum (EAIF) welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the European Commission's consultation on its upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA). The proposal seeks to address manipulative design, algorithmic personalisation, the use of chatbots in consumer interactions, and other digital practices. Furthermore, there has been an evolving debate about "AI agents" and "Agentic AI".

section_path: Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: At this stage, the official consultation materials for the DFA do not explicitly refer to "AI agents" or define them as a regulatory category. Nevertheless, the concept of agentic AI has entered the policy debate around digital fairness and consumer protection. Several academic and industry stakeholders have suggested that these technologies could challenge existing consumer frameworks, prompting early discussions about possible regulatory responses.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: From our perspective, a technology-neutral approach in the DFA would achieve the goal of protecting consumers without fragmenting the existing legal framework. We urge the Commission to strengthen consumer protection under the Digital Fairness Act without creating a new, undefined legal category of "AI agents." The objectives behind considering AI agents can be met by smartly applying and reinforcing existing rules and by that ensuring that consumers are not misled or harmed by autonomous systems acting in the market. Introducing "AI agents" as a new category at this stage would risk creating legal uncertainty and establishing a de facto second AI regulation, which would undermine the coherence achieved by the AI Act's horizontal framework.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: In reaction to the debate, the European AI Forum intends to address the potential treatment of AI agents under the DFA and would like to urge caution against introducing AI Agents as a new legal category. Instead, it examines how these concepts are already reflected within existing EU legislation and how the DFA can best ensure consistency, legal certainty, and consumer trust.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: As discussions on the Digital Fairness Act increasingly reference concepts such as AI agents, it is important to clarify what this term contains before considering any

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: ^{1} E.g. Busch, 2025, LINK.

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: regulatory implications. In public debate, AI agents are often described as autonomous or semi-autonomous systems capable of acting on behalf of users, which is a terminology not yet formally defined within existing EU law.

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: The term AI agent (or Agentic AI) has also been described in academic literature as “AI systems that exhibit autonomous decision-making, goal-oriented behaviour, and continuous learning while interacting with dynamic environments”² In contrast to rule-based chatbots or scripted automation, AI agents are capable of perceiving digital environments, formulating plans, and executing sequences of actions to achieve a defined objective with minimal human intervention.

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: Technically, such systems combine advanced AI models, like LLMs, with agentic frameworks that enable reasoning, task decomposition, and interaction with external tools, data sources, or APIs. Summarized, AI agents function as intermediary systems between the user and the digital environment. Examples include personal shopping assistants that autonomously compare and purchase products, or travel-planning agents that book and reschedule services in real time. Unlike conventional AI applications limited to reactive output, agents can initiate actions and adapt behaviour dynamically based on changing input or feedback.

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: The underlying architectures of these AI agents systems vary. They include:

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: Simple or model-based reflex agents, which respond to stimuli according to predefined rules or an internal model of the environment,

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: Goal-based and utility-based agents, which evaluate multiple strategies and select the most efficient or beneficial course of action,

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: Learning agents, which iteratively refine their performance through experience or reinforcement mechanisms, and

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: Hierarchical or multi-agent systems, in which several specialised agents coordinate to accomplish complex composite tasks.

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: These architectures illustrate that AI agents are not a discrete technology but a continuum of autonomy and complexity within the broader definition of “AI systems” (Art. 3 (1) AI Act). Given this wide spectrum of architectures and capabilities, it would neither be technically feasible nor legally sound to define “AI agents” as a single, regulated category within a consumer protection instrument such as the Digital Fairness Act.

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

evidence_quotes.quote: Proponents of including AI agents in the DFA treat agentic AI as if it were a clearly identifiable class of technology with specific, uniform characteristics.
In reality it is not a product but a technological concept.

paragraphs.text: Proponents of including AI agents in the DFA treat agentic AI as if it were a clearly identifiable class of technology with specific, uniform characteristics. In reality it is not a product but a technological concept. AI agents can be built on hundreds of different

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: frameworks and each enable different degrees of autonomy, reasoning, and tool integration. These systems are also highly context-dependent: the same base architecture may behave entirely differently when deployed in banking, e-commerce, or conversational settings.

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: However, the term "AI agent" itself remains technically fluid and legally undefined. It is used inconsistently across academic and industrial sources and does not appear in any existing EU legislative text. This lack of terminological precision is non-trivial from a regulatory perspective: premature use of "AI agents" as a legal category in consumer protection law could generate interpretive uncertainty and fragmentation across the Member States. For the purposes of the Digital Fairness Act, we advise to treat AI agents as a functional subset of AI systems already covered by the AI Act rather than as a novel regulatory category. Any reference should remain technology-neutral, describing the function (e.g. autonomous digital systems acting on behalf of users) rather than coining a new legal concept.

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, a separate AI agent regime would duplicate or conflict with the AI Act, which already governs AI systems, including those interacting with consumers. The AI Act mandates transparency (Art. 50 AI Act) and prohibits manipulative AI practices (Art. 5 AI Act). If the DFA were to impose similar obligations (e.g. requiring agents to identify themselves) without mirroring the same exceptions and scope, SMEs and European startups especially could face redundant or inconsistent compliance obligations. Such fragmentation would undermine the AI Act's harmonised approach and risk reintroducing a patchwork of national interpretations. Enforcement overlaps between AI supervisory authorities and consumer protection bodies could further complicate accountability and raise compliance costs, particularly for SMEs.

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty
2. Overlap with existing Legal Norms

evidence_quotes.quote: A distinct legal category of "AI agent" would generate legal uncertainty, overlap with the AI Act, and would create unnecessary regulatory burden.
Introducing a new "AI agent" layer would merely add administrative duplication without improving consumer outcomes.
The DFA should instead address AI-mediated consumer interactions through principles-based rules within the existing framework of the AI Act and consumer law.

paragraphs.text: Many of the intended protections of the DFA, like transparency, fairness and liability, are already covered by the AI Act, the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive, and forthcoming initiatives like an AI Liability Directive. Introducing a new "AI agent" layer would merely add administrative duplication without improving consumer outcomes. For startups and SMEs, uncertain definitions and multiple compliance tracks would act as an obstacle to responsible AI deployment, counter to the DFA's goal of fair and innovative digital markets. A distinct legal category of "AI agent" would generate legal uncertainty, overlap with the AI Act, and would create unnecessary regulatory burden. The DFA should instead address AI-mediated consumer interactions through principles-based rules within the existing framework of the AI Act and consumer law.

section_path: 1. Definition of AI Agents and Legal Uncertainty
2. Overlap with existing Legal Norms

paragraphs.text: The European AI Forum (EAIF) is the umbrella organisation of 13 national European AI associations and clusters. Combined, we represent over 3000 members, making us the largest European AI organisation. We represent members such as SMEs, corporates, organisations and individual experts in AI.
